content: The value of $\int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{(\mathrm{x}+1)(\mathrm{x}+2)}$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} \int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{(\mathrm{x}+1)(\mathrm{x}+2)} & =\int \frac{(\mathrm{x}+2)-(\mathrm{x}+1)}{(\mathrm{x}+1)(\mathrm{x}+2)} \mathrm{dx} \\ & =\int \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}+1} \mathrm{dx}-\int \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}+2} \mathrm{dx} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=\log \left|\frac{\mathrm{x}+1}{\mathrm{x}+2}\right|+\mathrm{c} $$

content: $\int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}^2\left(\mathrm{x}^4+1\right)^{3 / 4}}$ equals
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}^2\left(\mathrm{x}^4+1\right)^{3 / 4}} \\ & =\int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}^5\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{X}^4}\right)^{3 / 4}} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & 1+\frac{1}{x^4}=T \\ & -4 x^{-5} d x=d t \end{aligned} $$

$$ =-\frac{1}{4} \int \mathrm{t}^{-3 / 4} \mathrm{dt}=-\frac{1}{4} \frac{\mathrm{t}^{1 / 4}}{\frac{1}{4}}+\mathrm{c}=-\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^4}\right)^{1 / 4} $$

content: $$\int \frac{\sin x}{3+4 \cos ^2 x} d x$$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \because I & =\int \frac{\sin x}{3+4 \cos ^2 x} d x \\ & =\frac{\sin x}{3\left[1+\left(\frac{2 \cos x}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2\right]^2} d x \end{aligned}$$

put $t=\frac{2 \cos x}{\sqrt{3}}$

$$\begin{aligned} d t & =\frac{-2 \sin x}{\sqrt{3}} d x \\ I & =\int-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\left(3 t^2+3\right)} d t \\ & =-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{3}} \int \frac{1}{1+t^2} d t \\ & =-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}(t)+C \\ & =\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \cos x}{\sqrt{3}}\right)+C \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\int \frac{1}{x\left[6(\log x)^2+7 \log x+2\right]} d x \text { is }$$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} I & =\int \frac{1}{x\left[6(\log x)^2+7 \log x+2\right]} d x \\ & =\int \frac{d t}{6 t^2+7 t+2}\left[\begin{array}{l} \because \text { put } t=\log x \\ d t=\frac{1}{x} d x \end{array}\right] \\ & =\int \frac{d t}{(3 t+2)(2 t+1)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned} \text { Now, } \frac{1}{(3 t+2)(2 t+1)} & =\frac{A}{(3 t+2)}+\frac{B}{(2 t+1)} \\ & =\frac{A(2 t+1)+B(3 t+2)}{(3 t+2)(2 t+1)} \\ \Rightarrow \quad 1 & =A(2 t+1)+B(3 t+2) \\ \text { Now, on putting } t & =\frac{-1}{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad 1=0+B\left[3\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)+2\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \quad B=2 \\ & \text { and put } t=\frac{-2}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 1=A\left(2 \times\left(-\frac{2}{3}\right)+1\right)+0 \Rightarrow 1=A\left(-\frac{4}{3}+1\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad A=-3 \\ & \therefore \quad I=\int\left(\frac{-3}{3 t+2}+\frac{2}{2 t+1}\right) d t \\ & =\frac{-3 \log |3 t+2|}{3}+2 \frac{\log |2 t+1|}{2}+C \\ & =-\log |3 t+2|+\log |2 t+1|+C \\ & =\log \left|\frac{2 t+1}{3 t+2}\right|+C \\ & =\log \left|\frac{2 \log x+1}{3 \log x+2}\right|+C \quad[\because t=\log x] \end{aligned}$$

content: $\int \frac{\sin \frac{3 x}{2}}{\sin \frac{x}{2}} d x$ is
explanation:
$I=\int \frac{\sin \left(\frac{5 x}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)} d x$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\int \frac{2 \sin \left(\frac{5 x}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)}{\sin x} d x=\int \frac{\sin 3 x+\sin 2 x}{\sin x} d x \\ & =\int \frac{\left(3 \sin x-4 \sin ^3 x\right)+2 \sin x \cos x}{\sin x} d x \\ & =\int\left(3-4 \sin ^2 x+2 \cos x\right) d x \\ & =\int(3-2(1-\cos 2 x)+2 \cos x) d x \\ & =\int(3-2+2 \cos 2 x+2 \cos x) d x \\ & =\int(1+2 \cos 2 x+2 \cos x) d x \\ & =x+\sin 2 x+2 \sin x+C \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\int \sqrt{\operatorname{cosec} x-\sin x} d x$$ is equals to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text { Let } \quad & I=\int \sqrt{\operatorname{cosec} x-\sin x} d x \\ & \qquad \begin{aligned} I & =\int \frac{\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 x}}{\sqrt{\sin x}} d x \\ & =\int \frac{\cos x}{\sqrt{\sin x}} d x \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

Put, $$\sin x-k$$

$$\cos x dx=dk$$

$$\begin{aligned} & I=\int \frac{d k}{\sqrt{k}}=\frac{k^{\frac{-1}{2}}+1}{\frac{-1}{2}+1}+C \\ & =\frac{k^{\frac{1}{2}}}{\frac{1}{2}}+C=2 \sqrt{k}+C \\ & I=2 \sqrt{\sin x}+C \quad [\because k=\sin x] \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\int \sqrt{5-2 x+x^2} d x$$ is equals to
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} \text { Let } \quad I & =\int \sqrt{5-2 x+x^2} d x \\ I & =\int \sqrt{(x-1)^2+(2)^2} \end{aligned}$$

We know that

$$\begin{aligned} {\left[\because \int \sqrt{x^2+a^2}=\right.} & \frac{x}{2} \sqrt{x^2+a^2} \\ & \left.+\frac{a^2}{2} \log \left|x+\sqrt{x^2+a^2}\right|\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{x-1}{2} \sqrt{5-2 x+x^2} \\ & \quad+2 \log \left|(x-1)+\sqrt{5-2 x+x^2}\right|+C \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\int \frac{1}{1+3 \sin ^2 x+8 \cos ^2 x} d x$$ is equals to
explanation:
Let $$I=\int \frac{1}{1+3 \sin ^2 x+8 \cos ^2 x} d x$$

Dividing the numerator and denominator by $$\cos ^2 x$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow I=\int \frac{\sec ^2 x}{\sec ^2 x+3 \tan ^2 x+8} d x \\ & \Rightarrow I=\int \frac{\sec ^2 x}{1+\tan ^2 x+3 \tan ^2 x+8} d x \\ & \Rightarrow I=\int \frac{\sec ^2 x}{4 \tan ^2 x+9} d x \end{aligned}$$

Putting, $$\tan x=t \Rightarrow \sec ^2 x d x=d t$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & I=\int \frac{d t}{4 t^2+9}=\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{d t}{t^2+\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2}+C \\ I & =\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{3 / 2} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{t}{3 / 2}\right)+C \\ \Rightarrow \quad I & =\frac{1}{6} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 t}{3}\right)+C=\frac{1}{6} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \tan x}{3}\right)+C \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\int \frac{\cos 2 x-\cos 2 \alpha}{\cos x-\cos \alpha} d x$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } I=\int \frac{\cos 2 x-\cos 2 \alpha}{\cos x-\cos \alpha} d x \\ & I=\int \frac{\left(2 \cos ^2 x-1\right)-\left(2 \cos ^2 \alpha-1\right)}{\cos x-\cos \alpha} d x \\ & I=2 \int \frac{\cos ^2 x-\cos ^2 \alpha}{\cos x-\cos \alpha} d x \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =2 \int \frac{(\cos x-\cos \alpha)(\cos x+\cos \alpha)}{(\cos x-\cos \alpha)} d x \\ & =2 \int(\cos x+\cos \alpha) d x=2(\sin x+x \cos \alpha)+c \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\int \frac{d x}{(x+2)\left(x^2+1\right)}=a \log \left|1+x^2\right|+b \tan ^{-1} x +\frac{1}{5} \log |x+2|+c,$$ then
explanation:
Given,

$$\int \frac{d x}{(x+2)\left(x^2+1\right)}=a \log \left|1+x^2\right|+b \tan ^{-1} x +\frac{1}{5} \log |x+2|+c$$ .... (i)

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \frac{1}{(x+2)\left(x^2+1\right)}=\frac{A}{(x+2)}+\frac{B x+C}{\left(x^2+1\right)} \\ & \therefore \quad 1=A\left(x^2+1\right)+(x+2)(B x+C) \\ & \quad 1=A x^2+A+B x^2+C x+2 B x+2 C \\ & \quad 1=(A+B) x^2+x(2 B+C)+A+2 C \end{aligned}$$

Here, $$A+B=0$$,

$$2 B+C=0 \text { and } A+2 C=1$$

On solving them, we get

$$A=\frac{1}{5}, B=\frac{-1}{5} \text { and } C=\frac{2}{5}$$

Therefore, $$\int \frac{d x}{(x+2)\left(x^2+1\right)}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\int\left(\frac{1}{5(x+2)}+\frac{\left.\frac{-1}{5} x+\frac{2}{5}\right.}{x^2+1}\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{5} \int \frac{1}{(x+2)} d x-\frac{1}{5} \int \frac{x}{x^2+1} d x+\frac{2}{5} \int \frac{d x}{1+x^2} \\ & =\frac{1}{5} \log |x+2|-\frac{1}{10} \log \left|1+x^2\right|+\frac{2}{5} \tan ^{-1} x+c \\ & =-\frac{1}{10} \log \left(1+x^2\right)+\frac{2}{5} \tan ^{-1} x+\frac{1}{5} \log |x+2|+c \quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

On comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$a=\frac{-1}{10} \text { and } b=\frac{2}{5}$$

content: $$\int \frac{x^3 \sin \left(\tan ^{-1}\left(x^4\right)\right)}{1+x^8} d x$$ is equal to
explanation:
Let $$I=\int \frac{x^3 \sin \left[\tan ^{-1}\left(x^4\right)\right]}{1+x^8}$$

Let $$\tan ^{-1}\left(x^4\right)=t$$.

On differentiating w.r.t. $$t$$, we get

$$\begin{gathered} \frac{1}{1+x^8} \times 4 x^3 d x=d t \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{x^3}{1+x^8} d x=\frac{1}{4} d t \\ I=\frac{1}{4} \int \sin t d t \end{gathered}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =-\frac{1}{4} \cos t+C \\ & =-\frac{1}{4} \cos \left(\tan ^{-1}\left(x^4\right)\right)+C \end{aligned}$$

content: The value of $$\int \frac{x^2 d x}{\sqrt{x^6+a^6}}$$ is equal to
explanation:
Let $$I=\int \frac{x^2}{\sqrt{x^6+a^6}} d x$$

Let $$x^3=t$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad 3 x^2 d x=d t \\ & I=\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{1}{\sqrt{t^2+\left(a^3\right)^2}} d t \\ &=\frac{1}{3} \log \left|t+\sqrt{t^2+a^6}\right|+C \\ &=\frac{1}{3} \log \left|x^3+\sqrt{x^6+a^6}\right|+C \end{aligned}$$

content: The value of $$\int \frac{x e^x d x}{(1+x)^2}$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text { Let } I & =\int \frac{x e^x d x}{(1+x)^2} \\ & =\int \frac{e^x(x+1-1)}{(1+x)^2} d x \\ & =\int e^x\left[\frac{1}{1+x}+\left(\frac{-1}{(1+x)^2}\right)\right] d x \end{aligned}$$

Let $$\frac{1}{1+x}=f(x)$$

$$\therefore \quad f^{\prime}(x)=-\frac{1}{(1+x)^2}$$

Using the formula, $$\int e^x\left[f(x)+f^{\prime}(x)\right] d x$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =e^x f(x)+C \\ \Rightarrow \quad I & =e^x\left(\frac{1}{1+x}\right)+C=\frac{e^x}{1+x}+C \end{aligned}$$

content: The value of $$\int e^x\left[\frac{1+\sin x}{1+\cos x}\right] d x$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text { Let } I & =\int e^x\left(\frac{1+\sin x}{1+\cos x}\right) d x \\ & =\int e^x\left(\frac{1+2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}{2 \cos ^2 \frac{x}{2}}\right) d x \\ & =\int e^x\left[\frac{\sec ^2 \frac{x}{2}}{2}+\tan \frac{x}{2}\right] d x \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \quad f(x)=\tan \frac{x}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\sec ^2 \frac{x}{2}}{2} \end{aligned}$$

Using the formula, $$\int e^x\left[f(x)+f^{\prime}(x)\right] d x$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =e^x f(x)+C \\ \Rightarrow \quad I & =e^x \times \tan \frac{x}{2}+C \end{aligned}$$

content: The value of $$\int \frac{1+x^4}{1+x^6} d x$$ is
explanation:
We have, $$\int \frac{1+x^4}{1+x^6} d x$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\int \frac{1+x^4}{1+x^6} \times \frac{1+x^2}{1+x^2} d x \\ & =\int \frac{1+x^2+x^4+x^6}{\left(1+x^6\right)} d x \\ & =\int \frac{1+x^6}{\left(1+x^6\right) 1+x^2} d x+\int \frac{x^2\left(1+x^2\right)}{\left(1+x^6\right)\left(1+x^2\right)} d x \\ & =\int \frac{d x}{1+x^2}+\int \frac{x^2}{1+x^6} d x \\ & =\tan ^{-1} x+\int \frac{x^2}{1+\left(x^3\right)^2} d x \\ & =\tan ^{-1} x+\int \frac{d t / 3}{1+t^2} \\ \end{aligned}$$

Put $$x^3=t$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad 3 x^2 d x & =d t \\ \Rightarrow \quad x^2 d x & =\frac{d t}{3} \\ & =\tan ^{-1} x+\frac{1}{3} \tan ^{-1}(t)+C \\ & =\tan ^{-1} x+\frac{1}{3} \tan ^{-1}\left(x^3\right)+C \end{aligned}$$

content: The value of $$\int e^{\sin x} \sin 2 x d x$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned} \text { We have, } & \int e^{\sin x} \sin 2 x d x \\ &= \int e^{\sin x} \cdot(2 \sin x \cos x) d x \\ &= 2 \int e^{\sin x} \sin x \cos x d x \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { let } \sin x=t & \\ \Rightarrow \cos x d x & =d t=2 \int e^t \cdot t d t \\ & =2\left[t \int e^t d t-\int\left(\frac{d}{d t}(t) \int e^t d t\right) d t\right] \\ & =2\left[t \cdot e^t-\int e^t d t\right] \\ & =2\left[t \cdot e^t-e^t\right]+C \\ & =2\left[e^t(t-1)\right]+C \\ & =2\left[e^{\sin x}(\sin x-1)\right]+C \\ & =2 e^{\sin x}(\sin x-1)+C \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\int \frac{3 x+1}{(x-1)(x-2)(x-3)} d x A \log |x-1| B \log |x-2|+C \log |x-3|+C$$, then the values of $$A, B$$ and $$C$$ are respectively
explanation:
We have,

$$\int \frac{3 x+1}{(x-1)(x-2)(x-3)} d x$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \frac{3 x+1}{(x-1)(x-2)(x-3)}=\frac{A}{x-1}+\frac{B}{x-2}+\frac{C}{x-3} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad(3 x+1)=A(x-2)(x-3)+B(x-1)(x-3) +C(x-1)(x-2) \\ & \text { put } x-1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x=1 \\ & \text { Then, } 3 \times 1+1=A(-1)(-2) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 4=2 A \\ & \Rightarrow \quad A=2 \\ & \text { Put } \quad x-2=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x=2 \\ & \text { Then, } 7=B(2-1)(2-3) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 7=B(1)(-1) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad B=-7 \\ & \text { And put } x-3=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x=3 \\ & \text { Then, } 10=C(3-1)(3-2) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 10=C(2)(1) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad C=5 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \frac{3 x+1}{(x-1)(x-2)(x-3)}=\frac{2}{x-1}-\frac{7}{x-2}+\frac{5}{x-3} \\ & \therefore \int \frac{3 x+1}{(x-1)(x-2)(x-3)} d x \\ & \quad=\int \frac{2}{x-1} d x-\int \frac{7}{x-2} d x+\int \frac{5}{x-3} d x \\ & \Rightarrow \int \frac{3 x+1}{(x-1)(x-2)(x-3)} d x \\ & =2 \log |x-1|-7 \log |x-2|+5 \log |x-3|+C \\ & =A \log |x-1|+B \log |x-2|+C \log |x-3|+C \text { (Given) } \\ & \Rightarrow A=2, B=-7, C=5 \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\int x^3 \sin 3 x d x=$$
explanation:
key Idea Use Integration by parts as

$$\begin{aligned} & \int u \cdot v d x=u \int v d x-\int\left(\frac{d}{d x}(u) \int v d x\right) d x \\ & \Rightarrow x^3 \int \sin 3 x d x-\int\left(\frac{d}{d x}\left(x^3\right) \int \sin 3 x d x\right) d x \\ & \Rightarrow x^3\left(\frac{-\cos 3 x}{3}\right)-\int\left(3 x^2\right)\left(-\frac{\cos 3 x}{3}\right) d x \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-x^3 \cos 3 x}{3}+\int x^2 \cos 3 x d x \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow-\frac{x^3 \cos 3 x}{3}+\left[\begin{array}{r} x^2 \int \cos 3 x d x-\int\left(\frac{d}{d x}\left(x^2\right)\right) \\ \int \cos 3 x d x \end{array}\right] d x \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-x^3 \cos 3 x}{3}+\left[\frac{x^2 \sin 3 x}{3}-\int\left(2 x \cdot \frac{\sin 3 x}{3}\right) d x\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-x^2 \cos 3 x}{3}+\frac{1}{3}\left[x^2 \sin 3 x-2 \int x \sin 3 x d x\right] \\ \end{aligned}$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{-x^2 \cos 3 x}{3}+\frac{1}{3} \left[x^2 \sin 3 x-2\left\{x \int \sin 3 x d x-\int\left(\frac{d}{d x}(x) \int \sin 3 x d x\right) d x\right]\right.$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{-x^2 \cos 3 x}{3}+\frac{1}{3}\left[x^2 \sin 3 x-2\left\{\begin{array}{l} x\left(\frac{-\cos 3 x}{3}\right) \\ -\int\left(\frac{-\cos 3 x}{3}\right) \end{array}\right\}\right]+C$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{-x^2 \cos 3 x}{3}+\frac{x^2 \sin 3 x}{3}+\frac{2 x \cos 3 x}{9}-\frac{2 \sin 3 x}{27}+C$$

content: $$\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{x}+x \sqrt{x}} d x=$$
explanation:
Let $$I=\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{x}(x+1)}$$

put $$\sqrt{x}=t \Rightarrow x=t^2 \Rightarrow d x=2 t d t$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad I & =\int \frac{2 t d t}{t\left(t^2+1\right)}=2 \int \frac{d t}{t^2+1}=2 \tan ^{-1} t+c \\ & =2 \tan ^{-1} \sqrt{x}+c \quad(\text { since, } t=\sqrt{x}) \end{aligned}$$

content:
$$\begin{aligned} & \int \frac{2 x-1}{(x-1)(x+2)(x-3)} d x \\ & \quad=A \log |x-1|+B \log |x+2|+C \log |x-3|+K \end{aligned}$$

Then $$A, B, C$$ are respectively
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { We have } \int \frac{2 x-1}{(x-1)(x+2)(x-3)} d x \\ & =A \log |x-1|+B \log |x+2|+C \log |x-3|+K \end{aligned}$$

$$\text { Let, } \frac{2 x-1}{(x-1)(x+2)(x-3)}=\frac{A}{x-1}+\frac{B}{x+2}+\frac{C}{x-3}$$

$$\begin{gathered} \Rightarrow 2 x-1=A(x+2)(x-3)+B(x-1) \\ (x-3)+C(x-1)(x+2) \end{gathered}$$

put $$x-1=0 \Rightarrow x=1$$ then, $$2 \times 1-1=A(3)(-2)$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad A=-\frac{1}{6}$$

put $$x+2=0 \Rightarrow x=-2$$

then, $$2(-2)-1=B(-2-1)(-2-3) \Rightarrow B=-\frac{1}{3}$$

put $$x-3=0 \Rightarrow x=3$$

then, $$2 \times 3-1=c(3-1)(3-2) \Rightarrow c=\frac{1}{2}$$

content: $\int \frac{1}{1+e^x} d x$ is equal to
explanation:
Let's evaluate the integral:

$ I = \int \frac{1}{1+e^x} \, dx $

First, rewrite the integrand:

$ I = \int \frac{e^{-x}}{e^{-x} + 1} \, dx $

Now, use the substitution method. Let:

$ u = e^{-x} + 1 $

Differentiating both sides with respect to $ x $, we get:

$ \frac{du}{dx} = -e^{-x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad -e^{-x} \, dx = du $

Substituting, the integral becomes:

$ I = -\int \frac{1}{u} \, du $

This integral simplifies to:

$ I = -\log |u| + C $

Substitute back $ u = e^{-x} + 1 $:

$ I = -\log (e^{-x} + 1) + C $

Using the properties of logarithms:

$ I = \log \left(\frac{1}{e^{-x} + 1}\right) + C $

Rewriting $ \frac{1}{e^{-x} + 1} $ in terms of $ e^x $:

$ I = \log \left(\frac{e^x}{e^x + 1}\right) + C $

So, the final answer is:

$ I = \log \left(\frac{e^x}{e^x + 1}\right) + C $

content: $\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{3-6 x-9 x^2}} d x$ is equal to
explanation:
We need to evaluate the integral:

$ I = \int \frac{1}{\sqrt{3 - 6x - 9x^2}} \, dx $

First, let's rewrite the expression under the square root:

$ I = \int \frac{1}{\sqrt{3 - (9x^2 + 6x + 1) + 1}} \, dx $

Notice that $9x^2 + 6x + 1$ can be rewritten to form a perfect square. We have:

$ 9x^2 + 6x + 1 = (3x + 1)^2 $

Therefore, the integral becomes:

$ I = \int \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2)^2 - (3x + 1)^2}} \, dx $

Next, let's perform a substitution. Let:

$ 3x + 1 = t $

Then the differential becomes:

$ d(3x + 1) = dt \quad \implies \quad 3 \, dx = dt \quad \Rightarrow \quad dx = \frac{1}{3} \, dt $

Substituting these into the integral, we get:

$ I = \frac{1}{3} \int \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2)^2 - t^2}} \, dt $

The integral $\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{a^2 - t^2}} \, dt$ is recognized as the inverse sine function:

$ \int \frac{1}{\sqrt{a^2 - t^2}} \, dt = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{t}{a}\right) + C $

Applying this formula with $a = 2$, we have:

$ I = \frac{1}{3} \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{t}{2}\right) + C $

Substitute back $t = 3x + 1$:

$ I = \frac{1}{3} \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{3x + 1}{2}\right) + C $

content: $\int e^{\sin x} \cdot\left(\frac{\sin x+1}{\sec x}\right) d x$ is equal to
explanation:
To solve the integral $\int e^{\sin x} \cdot \left(\frac{\sin x + 1}{\sec x}\right) dx$, let's start with the given function:

$ I = \int e^{\sin x} \cdot \left(\frac{\sin x + 1}{\sec x}\right) dx $

This can be rewritten as:

$ I = \int e^{\sin x} \cdot (\sin x + 1) \cos x \, dx $

To simplify, we perform a substitution. Let $\sin x = t$. Then, the derivative is:

$ \cos x \, dx = dt $

Substituting these into the integral, we get:

$ I = \int e^t (t + 1) \, dt $

We recognize that this integral can be solved using the formula:

$ \int e^x \{f(x) + f'(x)\} \, dx = e^x f(x) + C $

Applying this formula with $f(x) = t$ and $f'(x) = 1$, we have:

$ I = t e^t + C $

Substituting back $t = \sin x$, we arrive at the solution:

$ I = \sin x \cdot e^{\sin x} + C $

content: $$ \int \sqrt{x^2+2 x+5} d x \text { is equal to } $$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Let } I=\int \sqrt{x^2+2 x+5} d x \\ &=\int \sqrt{(x+1)^2+4} d x \\ &=\int \sqrt{(x+1)^2+(2)^2} d x \\ &=\frac{x+1}{2} \sqrt{(x+1)^2+(2)^2}+\frac{(2)^2}{2} \log \mid x+1+\sqrt{\left.(x+1)^2+(2)^2\right)}+c \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} =\frac{x+1}{2} \sqrt{x^2+2 x+5}+2\log \left|x+1+\sqrt{x^2+2 x+5}\right|+c \end{aligned} $$

content: $\int \frac{(x+3) e^x}{(x+4)^2} d x$ is equal to
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} \text { Let } I & =\int \frac{x+3}{(x+4)^2} e^x d x \\ & =\int e^x\left[\frac{x+4-1}{(x+4)^2}\right] d x \\ & =\int e^x\left[\frac{1}{x+4}+\frac{-1}{(x+4)^2}\right] d x \\ & =\int e^x \frac{1}{x+4} d x+\int e^x\left(\frac{-1}{(x+4)^2}\right) d x \\ & =\frac{1}{X+4} e^x-\int e^x \cdot\left(\frac{-1}{(x+4)^2}\right) d x+ \int e^x\left(-\frac{1}{(x+4)^2}\right) d x\\ & =\frac{e^x}{x+4}+C \end{aligned} $$

content: $\int \frac{\cos 2 x-\cos 2 \theta}{\cos x-\cos \theta} d x$ is equal to
explanation: $$ \text { } \begin{aligned}Let \,\, I & =\int \frac{\cos 2 x-\cos 2 \theta}{\cos x-\cos \theta} d x \\ & =\int \frac{\left(2 \cos ^2 x-1\right)-\left(2 \cos ^2 \theta-1\right)}{\cos x-\cos \theta} d x \\ & =2 \int \frac{\cos ^2 x-\cos ^2 \theta}{\cos x-\cos \theta} d x \\ & =2 \int \frac{(\cos x-\cos \theta)(\cos x+\cos \theta)}{\cos x-\cos \theta} d x \\ & =2 \int(\cos x+\cos \theta) d x \\ & =2[\sin x+x \cos \theta]+C \end{aligned} $$
